(St. Augustine, Doct. prol. 7.) --- We must always have recourse to him, though we must use our best endeavours. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: In. Greek, "Reason is chief of every work, and counsel must go before every action." (Haydock) --- We must distrust our own lights.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Them. The heart gives birth to good or evil, which the tongue utters, Matthew 12:34., and 15:18. --- Soul. What will science, without charity, profit him, if he should even gain the world? (Matthew 16:26., and 1 Corinthians 13:1.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Hateful. His sophisms are soon discovered. Greek, "there is one wise, hateful in his discourses: he shall be devoid of all wisdom."

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Soul. Being replenished with wisdom, he communicates it to others, and obtains an eternal reward. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: But. Greek, "And." The wisest must soon die: Even the race of Israel shall end: But the virtuous shall be for ever remembered, ver. 29. (Haydock) --- The blessed in heaven live for ever. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Life. Attempt nothing above thy strength: or Greek, try what agrees with thy health or soul, (Calmet) and see thou give it not what is hurtful to it." (Haydock) --- St. Paul (1 Corinthians xi.) exhorts every one to prove or try himself and his own conscience. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Soul. "We must not dispute about tastes." (Haydock) --- They are as various as men's faces. All conditions do not suit every person.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Honour and pay, Proverbs 8:9. (Calmet) --- Greek adds, "with his fees." (Haydock) --- The health of body and soul must be regarded. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: King. Physicians were formerly kept at the king's expense. (Pliny, [Natural History?] 29:1.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Them, as he will all superstitious remedies. (Calmet) --- "A crowd of physicians killed Caesar," said Adrian, on his death-bed. They often try experiments, and kill with impunity, (Pliny 29:1.) --- Yet we must not condemn the prudent use of medicine. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Wood, of various sorts. (Grotius) --- Many suppose that he alludes to the miracle of Moses, (Exodus 15:25.) who thus was supernaturally informed (Calmet) of the effects of a certain wood. (Tirinus) (Menochius) (St. Augustine, q. lvii. in Ex.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: The. Greek, "that his or its virtue might be known by man."

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: These plants. --- And of. Greek, "Of these the maker of ointments shall compose a mixture and his works are not yet finished, when peace (health) from him appears on the face of the earth." (Haydock) --- Diseases flee before him; so powerful are his medicines, that the very smell effects a cure.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Thee. We must neither trust too much in physicians, (2 Paralipomenon 16:12.) nor despise them, as this would be tempting God.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Sin. All diseases are in consequence of original sin, and many are inflicted for actual transgressions, John 9:2., and 1 Corinthians 11:30., and Numbers 12:10. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Then. Greek, "as being no longer. Yet give," etc. (Haydock) --- Be converted and offer sacrifice, as if there were no hopes of life. Still do not despair. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Thou. Greek, "a sweet smell (success) shall be in their hands." (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Physician. This state is very troublesome, and a punishment of sin, ver. 10.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Burial. Thus we must shew our respect for the deceased. The Jews also prayed for them, 2 Machabees 12:42. (Calmet) --- "The care....and pomp of funerals are rather the consolations of survivors than of service to the dead." (St. Augustine, cura 2.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: For. Greek, "and shed burning tears of mourning, (18.) and lament for," etc. (Haydock) --- Sorrow would be unsuitable for the saints, and useless for the damned, who being rebellious to God do not deserve our lamentations. Yet nature dictates, and people expect we should allow something to the first emotions of sorrow, (Calmet) for a day or two; and we may receive the consolatory visits of our friends, during the seven days of mourning, Ecclesiasticus 22:13., and John 11:19. (St. Paulin. ad Pam.) --- To avoid the evil reports of men, we must shew ourselves to be really sorry; yet we must not suffer grief to hurt our health. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: And the, etc., is not in Greek. Excessive sorrow brings on sickness, and renders us unfit for any thing.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Aside, in solitude. --- Substance; or what the poor has to live on, will seem sweet or bitter according as he is affected with joy or grief.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: End. This is the chief utility derived from attending funerals.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Thee. A man on his death-bed, or already dead, is introduced speaking. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Him. Greek, "thyself." He is at rest, we hope; (Haydock) be thou so too. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: A scribe; that is, a doctor of the law, or a learned man. (Challoner) --- Sorrow (Haydock) and too much employment, are injurious to learning. (Calmet) --- Sapientiae otia negotia sunt. (St. Bernard, ser. 85. Cant.) --- When pastors have leisure, they would do well to write, in imitation of St. Paul, etc. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Work: engraving on gold, or silver, or on precious stones.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Sitting. Such was the ancient custom of smiths, (Calmet) who carried all their implements with them, and laboured with much pain. (Chardin.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Glazing. The earthen vessel would otherwise spoil all but oil and water. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Ancients. The Essenes (Josephus, Jewish Wars 1:7.) and Therapeuts (Philo, Comtemp.) were most famous for doing so. In general the Jews despise the learning of foreign nations: but some have applied themselves to it, particularly Philo, Josephus, etc., 1 Machabees 1:11., and 2 Machabees 6:11.) --- Prophets. These required the utmost study, before the coming of Christ, 1 Peter 1:11., and Daniel 9:9. (Calmet) --- The virtuous must follow both an active and contemplative life. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Sayings, or history of Abraham, etc. --- Parables. This was most in vogue, 3 Kings 10. --- But mechanics abstained from this study, Ecclesiasticus 38:38. (Calmet) --- To meditate well, 1. knowledge; 2. virtue; 3. humility; and 4. hope, are requisite. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Governor of the province or army. The most enlightened were chosen, like Joseph, Daniel 1:4., etc.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Men. Travelling was very requisite, (chap. 34:11.) and history records the actions of the bad as well as of the good, for the instruction of the public.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Pray. Diligence and prayer are the means to acquire wisdom, Ecclesiasticus 4:13.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Lord, being in a sort of rapture, and enlightened by Him.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Generation. With what care do we not preserve the Scriptures? etc.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Thousand. Children or other men. --- Rest, in death. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: With. Greek, "as the moon in the first quarter," dichomenia. The Vulgate has read Diomenia, "divine transport." The author declares that he was inspired. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: By. Greek, "Hear me, ye holy children." --- Offspring. Literally, "fruits." (Haydock) --- He speaks to the children of Israel, the people of God: whom he exhorts to bud forth and flourish with virtue. (Challoner)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Bring. Greek, "praise a canticle."

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Voice. Greek, "songs of your lips, (Haydock) and with ancient lyres," etc. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Waters. At the world's creation, or at the passage of the Israelites. [Exodus 14:21., and Josue 3:13.] (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Salvation. None can prevent the salvation of God's elect. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Wonderful, or new. (Calmet) --- The greatest miracles cost him nothing. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Time. The veil shall be withdrawn, and Providence will appear. Greek, "all things are made for their proper use."

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: That. Greek, "as he changed the waters into saltness," (Haydock) at Sodom, (Calmet) "his ways are plain for the saints: so to sinners they are stumbling-blocks," ver. 30. The Red Sea gave a passage to Israel, and overwhelmed the Egyptians. (Haydock) --- Him. God does all with grandeur. He poured his graces upon Israel, and overwhelmed the giants in the deluge. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Good and, is omitted in Greek. Before the fall, all was happiness. Now, the wicked have still some mixture of good, ver. 32. (Bossuet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Water and fire. These are requisite to prepare bread. The Romans refused them to the enemies of the state. --- Cluster. Greek, "blood," as [in] Deuteronomy 32:14. (Calmet) --- God gives the necessaries of life, which the virtuous use well, for their reward; and the wicked ill, to their ruin. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Evil. The good or bad use of them decides all, Wisdom 14:11., Romans 8:18., and Titus 1:15.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Spirits. Storms (Psalm 10:7., and 148:8.) good angels, (Genesis 19:11., and Isaias 37:36.) or rather devils: who, though created in holiness, fell, and became executioners of the wicked. (Calmet) --- They abused their free-will, and are eternally punished. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Destruction, and final ruin of the impious, or at the last day. (Calmet) --- Appease. God is pleased with the execution of justice. (Menochius) --- Yet the devils cannot obtain a reconciliation; neither do they act to please God, having their wills obstinately bent against his, though they be forced to obey. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Labour, "anxiety," ascholia. (Menochius) --- All misery (Calmet) is in consequence of original sin. (Worthington) --- The life of man is a trial, Job 7:1., and 14:1. --- Of all. The earth, (Calmet) which covers us, when all other things cast us away. (Pliny, [Natural History?] 2:63.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Purple. Literally, "hyacinth," (Haydock) or violet colour. Purple was more like scarlet.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Knowledge. He is disquieted by restless (Calmet) and frightful dreams, ver. 6. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Fear, or no reason for it. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Beast. Their instinct causes them to fear death, to fight, etc.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Moreover. Greek, "death," etc., are all destined for the wicked.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Out, and severely punished. (Calmet) --- They shall not appear in the book of life.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Rejoice. The corrupt judge once rejoiced: but now he shall mourn. (Haydock) --- The possessions and grandeur of the unjust shall end in a moment, like a clap of thunder, Psalm 36:36., and Proverbs 10:25. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: And make. Greek, "they are unclean," (Haydock) and will produce no fruit.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Weed. Literally, "verdure." Greek Achei, (Haydock) properly denotes the rush, papyrus, which grows on the banks of the Nile, (Genesis 41:2.; Calmet) and was used for fuel. (Alpin. 1:7.) (Matthew 6:30.) --- Thus the wicked will presently (Calmet) be treated. (Menochius)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Grace. The beneficent will produce fruits, like those of paradise, Genesis 2:8.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: That is. Greek, "and of one that." --- In it. Greek, "and more than both is one discovering a treasure," (Haydock) who finds himself rich, without labour or injustice.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Both. Thus Judith, Zenobia, etc., ennobled their husbands, Proverbs 31:10.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Wisdom. Virtue gives more content to the soul than corporal pleasures do to the body. It brings us near to God. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Green. "The sight of no colour is more agreeable to the eyes." (Pliny 37:1.) --- The Church is more excellent than the synagogue. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Husband. Their union is closer, and they can best assist each other.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Counsel. By this the Romans conquered the world, 1 Machabees 8:31., and Proverbs 11:14.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Heart, and make people presumptuous; while piety gives real courage, Psalm 26:1. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: It, or "him." (Haydock) --- The virtuous man is covered with glory.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Indigent. Greek, "live not by begging," being too lazy to work, or incurring this punishment for thy sins, Leviticus 26:16., Psalm cviii., and Deuteronomy 15:4. Involuntary poverty is a most severe scourge. (Calmet) --- All are bound to labour for what is needful; (Worthington) some by prayer, others by trade, etc. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Meat. Such is the condition of the parasite, (Calmet) and of the beggar. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Death is terrible to all, but most to those who live comfortably. (Calmet) --- O vita misero longa, felici brevis. (Seneca)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Sentence, pronounced on Adam and all his posterity. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Flesh. "It is a great consolation to share the fate of all." (Seneca, Provid. v.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: What. Greek, "why wouldst thou refuse to submit to the?" etc. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Life. Thou wilt not be asked how long, but how well thou hast lived. No one will then envy thy long life. (Calmet) --- It will be in vain to plead that the length or shortness of life has occasioned thy sins; for God does all with justice and for the best, if men would use rightly his benefits. (Worthington) --- Thou wilt not repine at having lived too short a time. (Menochius) --- An evil life will be alone condemned, Wisdom 5. (Du Hamel)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Ungodly. They adopt the wicked manners of their parents (Calmet) and companions, and thus become still more criminal. (Haydock) oetas parentum pejor avis tulit Nos nequiores, mox daturos Progeniem vitiosiorem. (Horace, 2:ode 6.) --- This was terribly verified in the three French assemblies, which overturned the Church and state. (The year of our Lord 1793.) See Barruel. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Father. He is rather their executioner, (Calmet) and would have been less cruel, if he had murdered them while they were innocent, Wisdom 12:10. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Portion. It would have been better for them never to have existed, Matthew 26:24. This [is] the case of heresiarchs. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: From. Greek, "go to destruction." (Haydock) --- It is their destination (chap. 40:11.; Calmet) and choice. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Body. The death of the body is bewailed in the just, but that of the soul also of wicked people calls for our tears. They will soon perish, Psalm 9:7.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Great. The concern which all have for a good name, is one of the strongest proofs of the souls' immortality, Proverbs 22:11.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Peace, with docility, (Matthew 11:25.) or in the midst of prosperity, be on your guard, Ecclesiasticus 20:32. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Have a shame, etc. That is to say, be ashamed of doing any of these things, which I am now going to mention: for though sometimes shamefacedness is not to be indulged, yet it is often good and necessary; as in the following cases. (Challoner) --- Disciples ought to esteem what their masters teach, though they do not perceive the reasonableness of what they assert. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Opinion. Grotius corrects the Greek, "It is not laudable to be incredulous in all."

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Mother. Thy misconduct seems to redound to their dishonour, as if they had not given thee a proper education.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: People. They will stone thee. Remember the fate of Roboam, 3 Kings xii. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: And of. Literally, "out of respect for the," etc. De veritate Dei et testamento. (Haydock) --- The same words occur [in] Ecclesiasticus 42:2.; whence Jansenius thinks they have been transferred hither. Most refer them to what goes before. Blush for lying, which is contrary to the truth of God; and for the other sins which injure his covenant. --- Meat. This posture betrays idleness or pride.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Men. Here the Greek concludes the former chapter very properly, (Haydock) as we must be ashamed of doing these things, and not of those which follow. (Calmet) --- "Of repeating [and] of the speech which thou hast heard, and of revealing secret words; (things.; Haydock) and thou shalt be truly bashful, (aischunteros.; Calmet) and find favour with every man." (Grabe's edition) (Haydock) --- Thereby. In such cases we must shew a holy impudence, like Jeremias 1:10, 18.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Covenant. We must be ashamed of transgressing these, (chap. 41:24.) but not of complying with them. This is the first duty of man. --- Ungodly. Blush not to oppose such a judgment, (Calmet) though all the judges should be against thee. Follow no collusion of man to do evil. (Haydock) --- Pass sentence without respect of persons, (Deuteronomy 1:16.; Calmet) whether the parties be thy companions, or only travellers, ver. 3. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Friends. Be not ashamed to leave something to thy friends, though relations may grumble; or execute thy friend's will exactly. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Little. Be scrupulously exact in thy dealings, but not afraid of advancing thy fortune by lawful means.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Buying. This regards magistrates, who must correct such frauds. Greek, "of the money (Calmet) to purchase from merchants." (Haydock) --- We may examine whether the coin be good, and strive to get things as cheap as we can. --- Bleed. Great severity was formerly used, Ecclesiasticus 30:12. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Keeping. Literally, "a seal." (Haydock) --- All must be locked up.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: In. Not out of distrust so much, as to keep servants honest. All who have written on economy give the same rules.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Men. So Daniel discovered the malice of the two ancients, Daniel 13:46. Admonish the aged not to enter into disputes, or strive with the young. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Knoweth. Literally, "who is hidden." (Haydock) --- An unmarried woman was styled Halma, or "hidden," Isaias 7:11. (Calmet) --- Pass away. Greek, "abuse." (Haydock) --- Some read adultera, (Sixtus V; Jansenius) instead of adulta, improperly. It was deemed shameful for a parent not to have his daughter married soon, 1 Corinthians 7:36. --- Hateful. Deuteronomy xxiv.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Barren. And thus fall into contempt, Deuteronomy 7:14. If she committed adultery she must die; or if she were only suspected, she must drink the waters of jealousy, Numbers 5:17. Husbands would often seek a divorce, on such occasions; which would involve the father in fresh difficulties.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Multitude, who will accuse thee of negligence. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Body's. Literally, "man's." But females are included in this term. (Haydock) --- It was also necessary to guard the Greeks, and pagans in general, against a criminal affection for boys.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Man. Greek, "woman." Malice is natural to her. Beauty becomes her ruin, as well as that of others. (Calmet) --- The Fathers compare women to a loadstone. (St. Basil) --- Men who come near them, are greatly exposed. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Better, etc. That is, there is, commonly speaking, less danger to be apprehended to the soul from the churlishness, or injuries we receive from men, than from the flattering favours and familiarity of women. (Challoner) --- Josephus (contra Apion ii.) attributes this sentence to Moses, and prefers the worst man to the best woman, as Euripides does in Clement. (Strom. iv.) (Calmet) --- But this is ridiculous. --- Reproach. This explains what sort of good the woman aforesaid has done; she had fostered the passions of men, and brought them to shame, which all the malevolence of an enemy could not have done. (Haydock) --- The company of women is therefore more dangerous, Ecclesiasticus 9. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Remember, or publish. He praises God and the great men of his nation, to the end of the book, (Calmet) or to Ecclesiasticus 50:29. (Haydock) --- By. Literally, "in." (Haydock) --- I have only abridged what the sacred Scriptures relate.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Work. The world displays God's glory and power, Psalm 18:6.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Saints, in heaven, or rather the Israelites, Psalm 147:20. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Signs. Literally, "sign," the order of the stars, etc. (Haydock) --- Yet God does not study these things, to discover what will happen. He is the author of nature. (Calmet) --- From his works we are to form some idea of his greatness, Job xxxviii., etc. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Another. By this opposition the world subsists, according to Linus, Epimenides, etc. Hanc Deus et melior litem natura diremit. ([Ovid,] Met. i.) See Ecclesiasticus 33:16.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Shew. We cannot behold these things without admiration of God.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: High. The sun is the most excellent of all irrational creatures, affording light (Worthington) and heat for the production of things; (Haydock) whence Aristotle (de Anima ii.) styles it "father of men and of gods." Yet it is inferior to man, being devoid of reason. All creatures manifest God's majesty; the little as well as the great. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Hastened. Some Greek copies read "slackened," alluding to Josue 10:13.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: In all. Complutensian Greek, "to stand guard." Other copies agree with the Vulgate, though embarrassed. --- World. To mark out the seasons, Genesis 1:14., and Psalm 103:19.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Day. The lunar system prevailed only after the captivity. The Passover was celebrated on the 14th of the moon of Nisan.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Name. Meni is used for the moon, (Jeremias 65:11.) which resembles the Greek men, "month." This is also derived from mene, "the moon." We know not what Hebrew words were used, Ecclesiasticus 6:23. The two former may both come from manah, "to divide."

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Armies. The stars, etc., are compared to a camp or army.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Judgment. The angels are not pure before God; (Job 4:18., and 15:15.) or rather the stars obey his orders, Baruch 3:34., Judges 5:20., and Psalm 118:91. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Brightness. Mille trahit varios adverso sole colores. (Virgil, Aeneid 5.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Judgment, to punish the wicked, Exodus 14:24., and Isaias 37:36. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Birds, sent to give information by letters, as the Egyptians still do. (Bochart 1:2.) (Isaias 60:8.) --- Tempests execute God's judgments, Job 38:22.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Broken, as from huge mountains, Job 38:39. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Strike. Greek, "upbraid, (Drusius) or make the earth fall in labour," odinesen. (Grabe) (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Lightning. Greek, "flying, he." (Haydock) --- Locusts. "They cover the cornfields with a destructive cloud." (Pliny, [Natural History?] 11:29.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Shower, as if God were about to drown the world. The melting of snow occasions dreadful inundations. (Calmet) --- Its whiteness sometimes deprives people of their sight. (Xenophon, Anab. iv.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Thistles. Greek, "stakes," pointed as it were with steel. (Menochius)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Crystal, or ice. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Burn. Cold destroys verdure no less than fire, Genesis 31:40.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Overpower it. Greek, "give joy; (25.) with," etc. (Haydock) --- Warmth and rain restore beauty to the earth, after the horrors of winter.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Still. Literally, "silent," Matthew 8:26. --- Lord. Roman Greek, "Jesus planted it;" which is a mistake for nesous, islands. (Calmet) --- "He planted islands in it." Grabe thus corrects the Alexandrian copy, though it agree with the Roman and Aldine editions. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Thereof. Psalm 106:23. "He who has not sailed has seen nothing evil." (Possidip.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Monstrous. Greek, "creation of whales." (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Is. Greek, "his angel gives a prosperous journey." --- Regulated. Greek, "blended." (Haydock) --- If God be angry, all will go to the bottom. (Grotius)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: All. Literally, "in all." Every thing obeys God, Ecclesiasticus 42:15., and Ecclesiastes 12:13.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Him. So the Greek reads. Vulgate seems to say, "boasting in all, what shall we be able to do?" What is man, fighting against the Lord! (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: His. Greek, "exalting the Lord, exert your power; and labour, for you will not come up to him: (35.) who," etc. (Haydock) --- He is above all praise, Psalm 144:3. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Greek prefixes, "A Hymn of the Fathers." The author here imitates the third work of Solomon. (Haydock) --- He had given rules for all virtues; and he now sets before us those who had put them in practice. (Calmet) --- It was the custom to sound forth the praises of these fathers in the temple and synagogues, ver. 15. (Grotius)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Glory. Greek adds, "in them." He was the author of all their virtue, (Haydock) and has acquired much glory by their means, (Calmet) among men; though his essential glory can never increase.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: In the. Greek, "futurity by their predictions." Kings, judges, and prophets are praised. (Haydock) --- The virtuous are justly styled kings, Genesis xxiii. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: The present. Greek, "the people by counsels, and by the knowledge of the office of scribes of the people. Wise in speech by their instruction. (5.) Seeking out," etc. (Haydock) --- Prudence and eloquence ought to appear in a ruler.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Tunes. The Hebrews, and David in particular, were famous for music. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: In. Greek, "established in power, living," etc., (Haydock) like Abraham, Ezechias, etc. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Generations. Literally, "races of their nation." But this is omitted in Greek.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Born. Greek, "some of them have left a name," (Haydock) or posterity, Deuteronomy 25:6.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Them. This may be understood of the wicked Israelites, or of the sons of Abraham by Agar, etc.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Mercy. Assideans, (1 Paralipomenon 6:41., and 1 Machabees 7:13.) or true Israelites.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Peace. The Jews were very solicitous about this, Genesis 35:8. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Into. Greek, "a model of penance to past or future generations." (Haydock) --- The Latin fathers suppose that Henoch was translated to heaven, or to the earthly paradise. It is the tradition both of Jews and of Christians that he is still alive, and will come to oppose Antichrist, (Apocalypse 11:3., Genesis 5:22., and Hebrews 11:5.; Calmet) when he will preach penance, (Worthington) chiefly to the Gentiles, while Elias will address himself to the Jews. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: A. Greek, "an exchange," to save mankind, Genesis 6:8., and 8:21. (Calmet) --- Noe[Noah] was perfect, (Worthington) which does not exclude some human failings. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Multitude. His name implies as much. (Calmet) --- He was father of all who believed in Christ. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Faithful. He received the sign of circumcision, and was ready to sacrifice Israel. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: In his. Greek, "that the nations should be blessed in his posterity." --- Dust. Literally, "a heap." (Haydock) (Genesis 12:2., and 22:17.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Men. Pharao, Jethro, and the whole nation of the Hebrews, whom he governed as a father, being the mildest of men, Numbers 12:23.[3.?] His life was a continued miracle, and he was honoured more than any prophet, (ibid. vi.[Numbers 12:7.?], and Acts vii.; Calmet) seeing God's works more clearly, but not his substance, Exodus xxxiii. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Saints, patriarchs or angels, as he saw God face to face. --- Enemies. The Egyptians, Amalecites, and all who rebelled against God. --- Cease. Removing the scourges which he had inflicted. His serpent devoured those of the magicians, who were confounded before him.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Kings. Pharao, Og, etc. --- Glory, when he had passed by, Exodus 33:22.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Meekness, giving him these necessary qualifications to rule a rebellious people, ver. 1.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Heard. Greek, "made him hear his voice."

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Face, familiarly. --- Life, by observing which, the Hebrews might live. Thus the trees of life and of knowledge were a sort of remedy against death and ignorance, Genesis 2:9. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: His. Greek, "holy, like to himself his brother of the," etc. (Haydock) (Psalm 105:16.) --- He was the interpreter of Moses, and honoured with the high priesthood. Aaron and his posterity were bound to be faithful to the law, ver. 19. (Calmet) --- Their priesthood was to last till Christ appeared, a priest forever of the order of Melchisedech.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Robe. Literally, "stole," (Haydock) which was a long robe, used in the East by both sexes. --- Crowned. Greek, "confirmed" him in his dignity. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: To the. Greek, "enclosing his legs, (Haydock; breeches, Calmet) and the robe hanging down to the feet, (podere) and the garment over the shoulders," (Haydock, ephod) which met at the breast, where the rational was fixed. See Exodus xxviii. (Calmet) --- Many. Greek, "spheres, (Haydock; or pomegranates, Exodus 28:23.; Calmet) with many little bells," (Haydock) to admonish the people of his coming.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Man. Beseleel, who made the greatest part. (Calmet) --- Greek, "of an embroiderer, with the rational of judgment, and the signs (manifestation. Calmet) of truth. (Haydock) --- Thus the Septuagint commonly describe the Urim and Thummim, Exodus 28:6. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Tribes. Greek, "sons." (Haydock) --- Their names were engraven on 12 stones.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Holiness, or "holy to the Lord," Exodus xxviii. (Calmet) --- Work. Greek, "works very rich." (Haydock) --- This regards all his attire. The high priest only used it in the temple, on grand festivals. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: His. Complutensian Greek, "their." (Haydock) --- Some of the ordinary priests offered the daily holocausts.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Filled. Consecrated, Leviticus 8:26.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Testament. It prefigured that of Christ, which lasts for ever, Hebrews vii. (Calmet) --- Execute. Greek, "serve him, both by executing the priestly office, and by blessing the people," etc. (Haydock) --- This was one of the high priest's functions, Numbers 6:23. The rest might offer sacrifice and incense, except on the day of expiation, (Leviticus xvi.) and on solemn festivals, though the Scripture is silent on the latter head.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Law. The most important causes were to be tried before the high priest, who, with his brethren, were the interpreters of the law, Matthew 2:6.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Fire. Thus consuming 250 men, Numbers 16:30.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Eat. The skin of holocausts was alone given to the priests.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Jesus, the son of Nave. So Josue is named in the Greek Bibles. For Josue and Jesus signify the same thing, viz., a Saviour; (Challoner) and Nave is the Greek pronunciation of Nun; (Haydock) or this is an old fault of transcribers. (Calmet) --- Among. Greek, "in the prophecies." (Haydock) --- He was endued with the prophetic spirit, and was raised up by God as a noble figure of the great prophet, Deuteronomy 18:15. (Calmet) --- He succeeded Moses in the temporal power; the spiritual belonged to the sons of Aaron, Numbers xxvii. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Israel. He led the people into Chanaan, and divided it among them.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Hands. To pray, (Vatable) or fight, (Bossuet) or to give a signal, Josue 8:26. (Cornelius a Lapide) --- Stretched. Literally, threw (Haydock) darts, romphoeas.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: The. Greek, "he conducted the wars of the Lord." (Calmet) --- Other editions agree with us.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: In. Greek, "by his hand," (Haydock) and order, so that the day-light continued 24 hours.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Adversaries. The hail destroyed more than the sword, Josue 10:11.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Power. Greek, "complete armour for his warfare was before the Lord, since he," etc. They found it was impossible to resist such weapons. (Haydock) --- One. "Follow God," was a maxim of the ancient sages, which they even attributed to Apollo. Josue and Caleb did so in opposition to the rebels, Numbers 14:24. (Calmet) --- The rest all perished, (Worthington) if we except those who were not 20 years old, or who did not join in the murmurs of the seditious. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Mercy, and piety, (Calmet) endeavouring to prevent the murmurs of the people, which would draw upon them destruction, as the event shewed. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Land. Hebron, when he was 85 years old, Josue 14:6. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Lord. Abimelec must be excepted, (Calmet) as he was only an usurper. (Haydock) --- The Judges were all saved, though some have been great sinners. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Place. At the resurrection, which the Jews firmly believed, Ezechiel 37:3.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: People. Though he disapproved of their resolution, 1 Kings 8:6., and Osee 13:11.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: And the. Sixtus V, etc., read, "and he beheld the God," 1 Kings 3:7. (Calmet) --- Vulgate may signify also, "God beheld Jacob," (Greek) with a favourable eye, and enabled Samuel to restore their fallen state, and to recover the ark. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Because. Greek has not faithful, or what follows. (Calmet) --- Yet Grabe's copy retains pistos oraseos, "faithful in seeing" into futurity; so that his predictions were always verified, and he acquired the confidence of all, 1 Kings 3:20. Prophets were formerly styled seers: yet they could not behold God, as he inhabits light inaccessible, 1 Timothy 6:16. (Haydock) --- Light. St. James 1:17. seems to allude to this passage. (Menochius)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Without. Greek, "fed with milk," 1 Kings 7:6. The Philistines were discomfited, and continued peaceable during the rest of Samuel's government.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Nathan. Many other prophets lived at the same time. But he was most conspicuous, and crowned Solomon. (Calmet) --- Greek, "and after him Nathan arose to prophesy," (Haydock) and keep up the succession.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Flesh. Greek, "peace-offering." The fat was deemed most delicate.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Played. Tearing them to pieces with the greatest ease, 1 Kings 17:34. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Nation. Who were no longer under the control of the Philistines. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Thousand. This was sung in the cities, (1 Kings 18:7.; Calmet) and excited the envy of Saul, who nevertheless could not prevent David's exaltation. (Haydock) --- Did he, or the people who praised the Lord and David. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Horn. Power and glory. (Haydock) --- For ever, is not in Greek. David defeated the Philistines at the beginning of his reign. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Glory. Contained in the Psalms. (Haydock) --- He was always very grateful. (Calmet) --- His pure and sincere heart pleased God more than his other works. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Altar. Of holocausts. Moses had not appointed singing.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Set. Literally, "adorned the times." --- Of his. Greek, "while they praised his holy name, and sounded forth holiness early." (Haydock) --- David strove to give all perfection to the sacred ceremonies of religion, that the people might be attracted to the tabernacle, (Calmet) and serve the Lord with gladness. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Sins. Remitting the punishment, (2 Kings 12:13.; Calmet) and the guilt also, (Haydock) in consequence of his repentance. --- Covenant. Engaging that his posterity should enjoy the throne (2 Kings 7:12.; Calmet) if they continued faithful, Psalm 131:11., and supra [Ecclesiasticus] Ecclesiasticus 45:31.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Enemies. The piety of David was thus rewarded. (Haydock) --- It must have been the greatest satisfaction to him, to see so wise a son on the throne, and all in perfect peace, 3 Kings 1:48., and 4:24. (Calmet) --- These blessings were a reward of his virtue. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Youth. When other are less informed. This serves to place the apostacy of Solomon in a more striking light. (Calmet) --- The author seems astonished both at the wisdom and at the folly of this prince, (Haydock) and apostrophises him. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Earth. Or explored all the secrets of nature, retexit, 3 Kings 4:29.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Interpretations of riddles. Hence people praised the Lord, 3 Kings 10:24.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Copper, (aurichalcum.) Greek, "tin," 2 Paralipomenon 9:13.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Thyself. Literally, "thy thighs." Greek, "sides." Syriac, "strength." --- Subjection. Literally, "thou hadst power over thy body," (Haydock) using it as if it belonged not to God. He was guilty of excess in marrying a thousand wives, and yielding to idolatry, (Calmet) fearing to displease those whom he loved to his own ruin." (St. Augustine, de Gen. ad lit. 11:42.) --- Dreadful infatuation! (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Defiled. Consecrating some even to Moloch, (Pineda 7:11.) or having children by those whom the law prohibited, Deuteronomy 7:2., and 23:2. (Calmet) --- His sins were punished: yet God's mercy preserved his posterity. (Worthington) (Psalm lxxxviii.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Rebellious. Greek literally, "hard." (Haydock) --- Jeroboam was at the head of it, 3 Kings 12:14. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Lord. David's piety caused the sceptre to be still preserved in his family, (Haydock) though Solomon's conduct deserved to lose it. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Of the. Greek, "a root from him," to govern a part, while Jeroboam reigned over the other tribes of Jacob. (Haydock) --- God fulfilled his promises to Jacob and David. (Menochius)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Torch. He was animated with a burning zeal, (Menochius) like John the Baptist, admonishing kings, etc., John 5:35., and Luke 1:17. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: And they. Greek, "and by his zeal he diminished them. By," etc. (Haydock) --- He slew the priests of Baal, and many perished by famine, 4 Kings xviii. (Grotius) (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Heaven. That it should not rain for three years and a-half, 3 Kings 17:1. --- Thrice. First upon his sacrifice, and twice to destroy fifty soldiers, Ibid.[3 Kings xvii.] 38., and 4 Kings 1:10. (Calmet) (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Man. The son of the widow of Sarepta.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Destruction. The prophets are said to perform what they foretell; to indicate the certainty of the event. Elias denounced death to Achab, Jezabel, Ochozias, and the two Jorams, 4 Kings 9:12., and 2 Paralipomenon 21:12., etc. (Calmet) --- Brokest. Greek, "those in honour from," etc., alluding to (Haydock) Ochozias and Joram, 4 Kings 1:16., and 1 Paralipomenon 21:15. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Judgment. Greek, "the reprimand of the Lord," for betraying fear, (3 Kings 19:3, 9, 12.; Haydock) or the reprimand regarded Israel. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Penance. Greek, "reward," (Haydock) or punish the guilty. Hazael and Jehu were anointed by Eliseus, though by order of Elias. --- Thee. Eliseus was called from the plough, and adhered to his master, who governed the sons of the prophets in large communities.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Jacob. Protestants reject the authority of this book, partly (Haydock) on account of this (Worthington) ancient tradition. Enoch and Elias will appear in the days of antichrist, to convert many. The Jews will then agree with the Patriarchs, and embrace the religion of Christ. (Calmet) --- Elias came in the person of John the Baptist, at the first coming of our Saviour, but he will come in person before his second appearance. (Haydock) --- The application which Christ makes of the prediction of Malachy (Malachias 4:6.) to John the Baptist, does not exclude this more distant accomplishment. Elias is still alive in some part of the world, as well as Enoch, whom St. Augustine styles "the first-born (primogenitor) of our resurrection." (City of God 15:19.) (4 Kings 2:11.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Friendship. Like Eliseus, or the disciples of both. (Calmet) --- Greek, "who slept in love, for we also shall live indeed; (13.) Elias," etc. (Haydock) --- When we shall witness thy return, if we prove faithful, we may expect an immortal name, and not otherwise. (Calmet) --- He speaks in the person of those who shall live at that time. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Eliseus. Who received his double spirit, with his garment, 4 Kings 2:15. --- Prince. Joram, 4 Kings 3:14., and 6:32.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Overcome. Or "escape." (Greek; Syriac) He knew what Giezi and the Syrians were doing, at a distance, 4 Kings 5:26., and 6:12. --- Prophesied. Or raised the dead to life, 4 Kings 13:21. (Calmet) --- On his death-bed (ver. 15.) he also foretold the victory of Joas, ibid. 14.[4 Kings 13:14.?] (Vatable, etc.) --- The miracle wrought by his relics (Haydock) shewed that he was a true prophet. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Thereof. From Gehon, a fountain on the west, 2 Paralipomenon 32:30. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Lifted. Greek, "removed from Lachis, and, "etc., 4 Kings xviii., etc.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: He was. Greek, "and he purified (Haydock; or redeemed, Calmet) them by the hand (ministry) of Isaias. (24.) He," etc. (Haydock) --- Prayer prevailed when troops were insufficient, 4 Kings xix. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Sight. Greek, "vision." All his predictions were verified. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Perfumer. The Scripture reproaches this king alone with no fault. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Directed. Greek, "prosperous in converting the people," 4 Kings xxii., and 2 Paralipomenon xxxiv. (Haydock) --- He began when he was only eight years old: but set to work more effectually ten years afterwards. The prophets complain of the disorders which prevailed before that period. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Sinners. Or "sins," when iniquity was become fashionable. He consulted no false policy, (Haydock) but boldly exerted himself in God's service, and even invited the Israelites out of his dominions to come and celebrate the passover, 2 Paralipomenon 34:6. (Calmet) --- They belonged, in effect to him, as the twelve tribes were all promised to David's family, and the kingdom of Jeroboam was only a rebellion, Ecclesiasticus 47:23. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Sin. The two former effaced their sins by repentance, and never tolerated idolatry, which even Josaphat and Asa did, (3 Kings 22:43., etc.; Calmet) not destroying the high places. Many others yielded not to idol worship. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Despised. Greek, "they failed," and lost the crown.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: They gave. Achaz called in to his assistance the king of Assyria, who afterwards claimed the kingdom as tributary to him, 4 Kings 18:7, 14. Greek, "He (God, Calmet; Alexandrian copy has They) gave their horn," etc. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Holiness. Jerusalem, which was styled holy, in the sicles of Simon. --- Desolate. No one came to the festivals, Lamentations 1:4.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Renew. To foretell the destruction and establishment of kingdoms, Jeremias 1:5, 10.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Cherubims. Ezechiel 1:4., and 8:1., and 10:1.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Rain. Denoting great misery, Ezechiel 13:11., and 38:22. (Calmet) --- And of. Literally, "to do good," (Haydock) or to announce the liberation of the penitent captives, Ezechiel 18:21., and 23:16., etc.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Place. Ecclesiasticus 45:15. The Pharisees adorned the tombs of the prophets, Matthew 23:29. Daniel is not mentioned among them, because he lived at court.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Hand. Most precious, Jeremias 22:24., and Canticle of Canticles 8:6.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Josedec. He was a noble figure of Christ, and helped to build the temple, Aggeus 1:2., and 2:3., and Zacharias 3:1., and 6:11.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Houses. He laboured much for the people's welfare, 2 Esdras v.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Henoch. Before the deluge. (Menochius) --- He stood up for the Lord, amid the most corrupt people, and was translated, Ecclesiasticus 44:16. He and Joseph have many advantages over the rest. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Simon I was "styled the just, on account of his piety towards God, and his good-will to his countrymen." (Josephus, [Jewish Antiquities] 12:2.) Many apply what follows to him. (Eusebius; Salien; Menochius, etc.) --- But Simon II is more probably meant, who opposed the entrance of Philopator into the sanctuary, after he had offered sacrifice for him, in the pompous manner here described. (Bossuet) (iii. Machabees) (Calmet) --- Temple. Which began to want repairs. (Menochius) --- Simon II reigned twenty years. (Calmet) (Josephus, [Jewish Antiquities?] 12:4.) (Haydock) --- The first lived in the time of Ptolemy I of Egypt, when this book was written; and dies before it was translated, under Ptolemy III, about three hundred years before Christ. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Walls. Greek analemma, "the repairs (or elevated building, Mello. 2 Paralipomenon 22:5.) round the temple," including the court of the Gentiles, which had not been finished, Ezechiel 40:5. It was necessary to build at the edge of the mountain, (Calmet) and the wall was three hundred cubits from the bottom. This was more than double the height of the temple, as it was only one hundred and twenty cubits high. (Menochius)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Out. Of the reservoirs. But he repaired them. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Destruction. When it was in the most imminent danger from Philopater. (3 Machabees) (Bossuet) (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Enlarge. Greek, "fortify." --- Conversation. Greek, "when in the midst of the people, coming out of the house of the veil. (6.) He," etc. (Haydock) --- Leaving the most holy place, he was surrounded by crowds of sacred ministers, (ver. 14.) or rather on the day of expiation, he came into the court of the people, to purify them. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Cloud. He was a far superior to the other priests in majestic appearance and attire, as Lucifer is to other stars, etc., ver. 11. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: So did. Greek, "on the temple of the Most High," with dazzling effulgence. (Josephus, Jewish Wars 6:6.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Forth. Greek adds, "fruits." --- Rearing. Greek, "elevated to the clouds." (Haydock) --- Robe. Stole, or violet tunic. (Calmet) --- Power. That is, with all his vestments, denoting his dignity and authority, (Challoner) and extremely rich, as virtus often implies, Ecclesiasticus 44:3. (Calmet) --- Greek has, "boasting."

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Vesture. Greek, "enclosure of holiness," peribolen. (Haydock) --- His splendid attire of gold and jewels, cast a dazzling light all around. (Calmet) --- He was pleased to offer the peace-offering for the king of Egypt, (Bossuet) to honour him, though another priest might have done it. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Palm. A forest of young ones shoots up from its roots; (Job 29:18.) so the high priest shone among the other children of Aaron. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: King. Greek, "Almighty" King, pantokratopros. (Haydock) --- He presented an unblemished victim, with all due solemnity, Leviticus 3:1.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Grape. Pouring wine upon the fire, Exodus 29:40. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Prince. Greek, "universal king." (Haydock) --- The whole sacrifice was pleasing to him.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: God. Greek, "the Highest." He had required these sacrifices, as memorials of his covenant, and sovereign dominion, Leviticus 23:34.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: House. Greek (Complutensian) and Grabe, "sound, sweet melody was made."

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Office, leitourgian autou, "his service." (Haydock) --- The people prayed for king Ptolemy, to whom they were subject; though the high priest possessed almost sovereign power. The Egyptian monarch was present, (Calmet) and at the end of the sacrifice expressed his resolution to go into the inmost recesses of the temple, from which the people endeavoured to dissuade him. Finding their efforts to be in vain, "they all (addressing themselves to God) said with one accord." (3 Machabees) Grabe substitutes, "wherefore the high priest, Simon, kneeling down before the temple, and stretching forth his hand with solemnity, made this prayer."

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Prayer. Greek, "adoration, that they might receive a blessing from the Most High." (Haydock) --- Power. In keeping Philopator out of the temple. God granted his request, (Calmet. litaneios) "and scourged him who was so insolent and bold...throwing him like a reed, unable to move, and speechless on the pavement," 2 Machabees 2:26. After Philopator's guards had removed him, the high priest congratulated the people, (ver. 26.) expressing his abhorrence of their enemies in general, though he only mentions three neighbouring nations which had shewn a particular enmity to the Jews, when a contrary behaviour might have been expected, ver. 28. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Now. At the sight of these wonders, the author exhorts the people to be grateful, and full of hope. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: His, Simon's, (Menochius) or God's days. When he may judge it convenient. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Abhorreth. Viz., with a holy indignation as enemies of God and persecutors of his people. Such were then the Edomites, who abode in Mount Seir, the Philistines, and the Samaritans, who dwelt in Sichem, and had their schismatical temple in that neighbourhood. (Challoner) --- This was the source of continual dissensions. (Josephus, [Jewish Antiquities?] 11:8., and 12:2.) (John 4:20.) --- The Jews and Samaritans are still at variance. The former, in the days of Esdras, pronounced a curse against the latter, forbidding any of them to become converts to their religion, (Grotius) which, if true, shews a very blameable malice. See Deuteronomy 27:4. (Haydock) --- Which, etc., is omitted in Greek. These three nations had evinced the greatest hatred towards the Jews, and thus deserved to be accounted objects of horror, though if the author harboured any ill-will, his conduct is not praised, (Calmet) but only recorded. (Haydock) --- The Samaritans consisted of Assyrians, Jews, etc., and therefore are styled no nation; and they were foolish in mixing idolatry with the true worship. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Seir. Genesis 14:6. All the Greek copies read corruptly "of Samaria." --- Foolish. Thus Christ said, you adore what you know not, John 4:22. (Calmet) See 4 Kings 17:29. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Jesus. He declares his name, and concludes with wishing peace to the observers of these maxims. Grotius thinks that this was inserted by the grandson, (Calmet) who translated the work into Greek. (Haydock) --- But there is no reason for this supposition. Solomon puts his name at the beginning and at the end of Ecclesiastes, and he frequently commends his own instructions, as this author does, Ecclesiasticus 33:17,. and 39:16., and 51:19. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Sirach. This prayer may be in imitation of the book of Wisdom. (Haydock) --- Grotius assigns it to the grandson, without reason; as the author continues to speak of his travels, etc. He seems to have lived under Onias III and under Seleucus and Antiochus Epiphanes, of Syria, to whom he was probably accused, and hence took occasion to retire into Egypt, where Ptolemy Epiphanes was king. (Calmet) --- Others think (Haydock) that he was accused before Ptolemy Lagus, when he took Jerusalem. (Menochius) --- We know too little of his life to decide any thing. (Calmet) See Ecclesiasticus 50:1. (Haydock) --- Saviour. When we are not able to make a return, we must shew greater marks of gratitude, and particularly to God, for the favours which we have not deserved. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: By. To accuse me, Zacharias 3:1. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Gates. Greek, "many." (Haydock) --- He exaggerates the danger to which he was exposed, as he was not miraculously delivered from the flames, etc., (Calmet) though this might be the case. At least he thanks God for preserving him (Haydock) from the various torments which were then in use.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: In. Greek, "from the midst of a fire which I did not enkindle," or deserve by any crime.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Hell. The grave. I was preserved like Jonas. (Calmet) --- Unjust. Greek, "the accusation was made to the king, by an unjust tongue. My soul approached unto death, and my life," etc. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: World. Shewn to Noe[Noah], Joseph, etc. (Calmet) --- Nations. Greek, "enemies," ver. 12.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Thou. Greek, "I raised my supplicating voice from the earth, and I," etc. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: The Father. Grotius asserts boldly, that it should be, "my Father," and that some Christian has changed this. But his authority may be rejected, when he brings no proof; and Bossuet has solidly refuted him. The passage clearly proves the belief of the second person, and seems to allude to Psalm 109:1. (Drusius; Vatable, etc.) (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: When. He now gives some account of himself, and exhorts all to seek for wisdom. (Haydock) --- He was advanced in years, when he fell into the aforesaid danger, after his travels, Ecclesiasticus 34:9. (Calmet) --- Wisdom is most easily acquired, while a person is innocent, Ecclesiastes xii. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: After her. Being convinced that prayer is requisite for the preservation and improvement of such a treasure. See 3 Kings 3:8. (Calmet) --- And. Greek, "from the flower as of a ripening grape. My heart," etc.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Wisdom. Greek, "instruction for myself." (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Wrestled. Against pleasure, idleness, etc. This shews how arduous a task it is to attain wisdom. (Calmet) --- Confirmed. Greek, "in earnest."

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Knowledge. Greek (Alexandrian), "time of harvest." Grabe substitutes, "in purification," (Haydock) or the practice of virtue, Wisdom 1:4.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Heart. Or understanding, as the Hebrew place this in the heart, 3 Kings 3:9.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Her. I did it, with all solicitude, Canticle of Canticles 5:4., and Jeremias 4:19. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Tongue. Eloquence, (Menochius) which I will exert in praising him.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Year of the World about 3404, Year before Christ 600.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Babylonia. Septuagint, "Babylon," where Jechonias was in prison, (Haydock) now the 5th year, ver. 2. Baruch accompanied his brother Saraias, Jeremias 51:59. (Calmet) --- Cappellus supposes that the author confounded them together, which is not probable. (Houbigant)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Month, in which the king was taken. (Calmet) --- This was the fifth anniversary. (Haydock) --- Fire: but soon extinguished. (Usher) --- The taking of the city was effected at intervals, (Haydock) during eleven years, in the fifth of which this book was written. Jerusalem was at last reduced to ashes. As yet there were priests, a temple, etc., ver. 7, 14. (Worthington) (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Jechonias. He was in prison, where the Jews met to hear the words of Jeremias; (li. 59., etc.; Calmet) or the king might hear the letter in private. (Houbigant)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Sodi. Hebrew zud, (Haydock) means "pride," and probably denotes the great river Euphrates. (Calmet) --- Bochart reads Sur, (Houbigant) or Sori. The city Sura was there. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Joakim, or Eliacim, high priest under Manasses, etc., Isaias 22:20. (Calmet) --- The text only says that he was priest, and Cappellus asserts there was no pontiff then. (Houbigant)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Siven; a Syriac month, corresponding with our May. (Menochius) --- Vessels; or, "the vessels were of silver." (Haydock) --- By retrenching a, which, it would seem that they asked for the golden vessels. Sedecias had made silver ones to supply their place. These also might have been taken away. (Calmet) --- Baltassar had still those of gold, (Daniel 5:2.; Menochius) or at least some of them. (Haydock) --- Sedecias, now in favour, only asked for what he had made while a private man. (Houbigant)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: All. Cunctos should be vinclos, according to the Greek, "prisoners." (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Meat. Literally, "manna," as Septuagint express Hebrew monee; (Haydock) or as it is pointed, mincha; which properly means an "oblation" (Jeremias 17:26.; Menochius) of bread, wine, etc. (Calmet) --- And offerings. Literally, "offer." --- Greek has for sin before frankincense. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: King. Gratitude and duty prompted them, Jeremias 29:7., and 1 Timothy 2:2. --- Baltassar. Evilmerodac, his elder brother, was in disgrace. (Lyranus) (Jeremias 52:31.) (Calmet) --- Heaven, as long and prosperous as possible. (Haydock) --- It was a customary salutation to say, O king, live for ever, Daniel 2:4., and Psalm 88:30. (Calmet) --- Cappellus says Baltassar was not yet born. But how does he know this? It might be the title of the heir apparent, (Haydock) or of the king's sons. We need not decide in a point of such antiquity. (Houbigant)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Eyes, putting an end to our captivity, (Badwell) or causing us to detest our sins. (Calmet) --- Days. This they knew was God's decree, and they submit. (Haydock) --- They prefer serving the Chaldeans before other foreign nations. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Proper festival days. (Calmet) --- Hebrew mohed, or muad. They were still observed (Haydock) in the temple.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Curses against transgressors, Leviticus xxvi., and Daniel 9:11. (Calmet) --- Day. We feel the effects of sin. The land of Juda was not flourishing, though not yet laid waste. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Serve, or sacrifice unto: (Sanctius) operata Deo: (Tibul.) or to worship, Baruch 2:21. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Heaven. After the death of Josias, the nation was exposed to the greatest misery. Its kings were imprisoned by the Egyptians, and then by the Chaldeans; and surprising changes took place, all during the space of eight or nine years.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Daughter. This is not mentioned in sacred history to have happened when this was written, (Calmet) except in the siege of Samaria. Some of the captives of Israel might be now at Babylon, as the places to which they had been sent were under the same king; and they confess their common transgressions and chastisements. All distinction of the kingdoms was now at an end. Some might also have been reduced to this extremity, when Joakim or Jechonias were besieged and taken. (Haydock) --- We should not know that a similar distress prevailed under Sedecias, if it had not been specified [in] Lamentations 2:20., and 4:10. This prayer greatly resembles that of Daniel 9:4.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Kings of Egypt and Babylon, while the Ammonites, etc., derided us.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Under; a proverbial expression, Deuteronomy 28:13.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Watched, to punish. When he spares, he seems to slumber, Jeremias 44:27. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Justices. So God's law is frequently called, (Psalm cxviii.) because its observance makes us just. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: House: the temple, or from heaven. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Justice, etc. They that are in hell shall not give justice to God: that is, they shall not acknowledge and glorify his justice, as penitent sinners do upon earth. (Challoner) --- And all in the grave are incapable of making known God's perfections. An appeal to his glory is thus often made, Isaias 38:18., Psalm 113:17., and Ecclesiasticus 7:24.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Of evil is not in Greek. A soul which bears grandeur with pain, like Esther, and humbles itself before God, is most graciously received. (Haydock) --- Fail, by fasting. Such are the dispositions required for prayer, Psalm 68:3.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Fathers. Greek adds, "and kings." --- Prayers. Greek, "mercy." We boast not of our good works. (Haydock) --- Mercy may denote prayer or humiliation. Theodoret gives a better sense: (Calmet) "We trust not in the just works of our fathers, or of ourselves, to receive any pity." (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Serve, as Jeremias (xxv. 9., and 27:7., and 28:14.) repeatedly admonished.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Footstep. It shall become a desert, Isaias 33:8., and Lamentations 1:4.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Place. The soldiers ransacked the tombs in hopes of plunder. Joakim was left unburied. This would be very afflicting to the Jews, Jeremias 8:1., and 22:19., and 36:30. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Banishment. Syriac, "captivity;" (Theodoret) or "by pestilence inflicted by God:" apostole. (Grotius) (Jeremias 32:36.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Day. Its vessels were taken away, (chap. 1:8.) and few were left to attend. (Calmet) --- Grotius answers this difficulty. (Houbigant) --- The prophet foresees the event. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Heart, and be seriously converted, (Haydock) as the prophets had foretold, Osee 2:7., etc.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Neck. Literally, "back," insensible of stripes. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Thereof. Literally, "of them;" eis. But ei is more (Haydock) conformable to the Greek. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Another. Greek, "an everlasting covenant," (Haydock) by Christ, (Maldonat) prefigured by that which Nehemias renewed, 2 Esdras 9:38. (Calmet) --- The law of Moses was in force till Christ came. His law continues unto the end. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Everlastingly. Is such a short-lived creature an object worthy of thy eternal wrath? (Job 13:25., and 14:1., and Psalm 102:13.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Dead; banished, (ver. 11., and Ezechiel 37:5.) or of the ancient patriarchs. This motive is frequently urged, Exodus 32:13., Leviticus 26:41., Deuteronomy 9:27., Daniel 3:35., and Jeremias 15:1. (Calmet) --- People in sin and misery seem dead, (ver. 11.) yet may revive by grace. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Hand. Save us by thy power, and for thy own name's sake, Josue 7:9.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Offence. Greek, "debt." Parents might sell their children, Matthew 18:25. God threatens his rebellious people, that they shall be forced to borrow, Deuteronomy 28:44. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Hear. God replies. (Haydock) --- The Church has long read this has holy scripture, on the eves of Easter and Pentecost. (Worthington) --- This second part contains an instruction respecting true wisdom; which is to be found in God alone, (Calmet) and in the people to whom he is pleased to communicate it. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Grown. Houbigant, "growing." Five years had not elapsed: (Cappellus) yet they knew their captivity would be long. --- Dead. Some might yield to idolatry, Daniel 3:7, 12. (Haydock) --- They were in a manner buried, (Psalm 87:5.) and forced to dwell among people where they were exposed to many defilements, as if they had been near a dead body, Leviticus 11:25.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Peace. There is none for the wicked; but only for the observers of the law. --- For ever. Many read, "upon the land," conformably to the Greek.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Treasures? How few are truly wise and virtuous! The great men of the world have missed their aim. Israel has received the law. Yet where is the fruit of it? This is developed in the sequel.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Nations of Assyria? etc. They esteemed themselves as lords of nature, and delighted in hunting. The beasts and birds are subject to them, Genesis 1:26., Jeremias 27:6., and Daniel 2:38. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Unsearchable? Shall they not find the fruit of their works? (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Men; heirs of those conquerors, and equally in the dark. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Theman, the capital city of Edom. (Challoner) --- Eliphaz was king, Jeremias 49:7. --- The Phoenicians, so famous for industry, had no idea of true wisdom, no more than the Ismaelites, etc. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Agar, the mother of the Ismaelites. (Challoner) --- Merrha, probably in Arabia. --- Fables. This may be a reproach, or a commendation. Those nations delighted in enigmatical and sententious discourses, 3 Kings 10:1. (Calmet) --- Instructors of mankind deserve praise, not those who devise false gods for lucre. (St. Augustine, City of God 6:5., etc.) (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Possession. All the world belongs to him: yet how few partake of his wisdom!

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Giants, before and after the deluge, Genesis vi. Noe[Noah] and Israel were preferred before them.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Clouds? No human industry can procure this blessing. God alone is the dispenser of it, Deuteronomy 30:12. (Calmet) --- His grace makes the discovery easy, Matthew 11:30. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Evermore. Literally, "in the eternal time," or so long ago; (Haydock) designed to continue, while kingdoms shall change, Ecclesiastes 1:4.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Trembling. The sun stops, goes back, or withdraws its light, at his command, Josue 10:12., and 4 Kings 20:9., Matthew 27:45., and Job 36:30.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Watches. They are like his soldiers, Judges 5:20., and Ecclesiasticus 43:12. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Ever. True wisdom may be found in the law. It constitutes the happiness of Israel, Deuteronomy 4:7. Christ perfected and fulfilled it, Matthew 5:7. --- Life. Moses promised temporal blessings; (chap. 3:14., and Deuteronomy 30:15.) yet the faithful would be rewarded eternally. (Calmet) --- Wisdom is the law of God, Baruch 3:12. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Nation. Be faithful, lest another take thy place. This Christians have done, Deuteronomy 32:21. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Memorial. Greek literally, "O memorable Israel." (Haydock) --- Ye are left to support and restore the nation. This part of the letter is for their comfort.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Sold, like slaves, or people taken in war. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: God. Literally, "Him." Greek, "the Eternal." (Haydock) --- This is taken from Deuteronomy 32:15. (Calmet) --- Nursed you. The city is beautifully personified as a widow, ver. 12. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Near. Hebrew would be "daughters of Sion," ver. 14.

text: Children. Greek, "daughters." (Haydock) --- Many of both sexes were taken with Jechonias. (Calmet) --- Jerusalem comforts her children, and assures them of God's mercy. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Peace: the ornaments worn in my prosperity. --- Sackcloth, or a rough garment, used by penitents and in times of public distress.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Neck. Mardochai, Daniel, etc., were governors, Isaias 40:14. These predictions were more fully verified when the Gentiles embraced the gospel. (Calmet)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: When. Greek, "now ten times as much, being converted, seek him." (Haydock) --- The Jews became much more docile and attached to the law.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Named thee as his servant, spouse, (Calmet) and intimate friend.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: The. Greek Deilaioi. "Wretched those who have injured thee, and rejoiced in thy fall! Wretched the cities," etc. (Haydock)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: She; Babylon. (Challoner) --- Greek, "Wretched she," etc. (Haydock) --- Babylon shall fall a prey to Cyrus.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Fire of war. --- Devils. Whether they really dwell in ruins, or the people were of that opinion, (as the common sort are still) is not clear, Jeremias 50:39., and Isaias 34:14.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: East and west. From Babylon and from the islands, Isaias 11:11., and Zacharias 8:7.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Garment. Hebrew, "cloak," (Sanctius) such as were worn by people under no disgrace, Baruch 4:20. Thamar had one very costly, 2 Kings 13:18. (Calmet) --- Justice, or mercy. God is bound to fulfill his gracious promises. (Worthington)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: His. Greek, "thy." Christ, the sun of justice, would effect this, Isaias 60:1.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Piety. Thus shall Jerusalem be distinguished after the captivity; but still more so when Christ shall appear, and give peace to his Church, after the ages of persecution.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Arise. Baruch 4:36., and Isaias 52:2. --- God. They remember his law with pleasure.

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Children. Greek, "the throne." The return of the captives was a triumph, Isaias 46:20., and 49:22. They had abundance of horses, etc., 1 Esdras 2:66. Darius gave them a convoy of 1000 horsemen. (3 Esdras 5:2.) (Josephus, Antiquities 11:4.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Diligently, without stumbling under the pillar of God's protection, Isaias 40:3. (Calmet) Ad vos qua veniet, tumidi subsidite montes Et faciles curvis vallibus este viae. (Ovid, Am. 2:16.)

author: Rev. George Leo Haydock
text: Woods. This is a poetical description. They shall pass through the sands of Arabia, as if they were under a delightful shade.
